meta.name: quantum-proofing-blockchains
meta.description: A generalizable component taxonomy for assessing the quantum resistance of any blockchain. 5 core sections, 30 components, each with what it is and what it takes to be quantum-proof.

lens.signatures: Broken by Shor (ECDSA, EdDSA, Schnorr, sr25519, BLS). Fix: ML-DSA / SLH-DSA / FN-DSA.
lens.encryption: Broken by Shor (RSA, ECDH, TLS, IBE); harvest-now-decrypt-later. Fix: ML-KEM.
lens.pairings: Pairing-based crypto (BLS, KZG) broken by Shor. The single most common root cause.
lens.hash: Only weakened by Grover (quadratic, barely parallelizes). 256-bit hashes stay safe.
lens.depends: Can be built hash-based (safe) or pairing/EC-based (breakable); design decides.

meta.applicability_notes: universal = on essentially every chain; common = widespread but not protocol-core; conditional = architecture-specific (mainly rollups/modular/validity chains; mark N/A for monolithic L1s).

cores.name: Keys & Accounts
cores.label: Who's allowed to move money
cores.notes: Address is usually a hash of the public key, so untouched funds are safe; transacting reveals the key (Solana is the exception: address = raw Ed25519 pubkey). Migratability axis: EdDSA chains + Polkadot can rescue exposed accounts in place; Bitcoin/Ethereum BIP-32 wallets must freeze.

subsections.one_liner: The key that authorizes spending.
subsections.what_it_is: The user's secret key — whoever holds it can authorize transactions and move the account's funds.
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: Sign with a NIST PQ signature (ML-DSA, SLH-DSA; Falcon when finalized) instead of ECDSA/EdDSA/Stark-curve, usually via account abstraction.

subsections.one_liner: Where the private key lives.
subsections.what_it_is: How and where that private key is kept: a software wallet you control, a hardware device, a bank-grade HSM, or held for you by an exchange.
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: The stored key is a PQ key and the signer hardware/firmware can produce PQ signatures.

subsections.one_liner: One key split across several parties.
subsections.what_it_is: A single key split into shares held by different parties, who cooperate to sign without any one of them ever holding the whole key.
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: A threshold scheme over PQ primitives (research-stage; NIST IR 8214C). Splitting a classical key gives no quantum protection.

subsections.one_liner: Programmable account validation.
subsections.what_it_is: Accounts whose rules for approving a transaction are programmable smart-contract code rather than a fixed signature check (ERC-4337, native AA).
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: Implement a PQ-signature verifier. This is the main migration vehicle (per-account swap, no L1 hard fork).

subsections.one_liner: Device-bound or delegated keys.
subsections.what_it_is: Device-bound credentials like WebAuthn passkeys (the Face ID / fingerprint keys on your phone) and short-lived session keys that approve actions for a limited time.
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: PQ-capable authenticators or a PQ verifier in the account; P-256 is still Shor-broken.

subsections.one_liner: Names and IDs bound to an account.
subsections.what_it_is: Human-readable names and portable identities bound to an account — ENS names, decentralized identifiers (DIDs), and verifiable credentials.
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: Mostly inherits the controlling account's signature (safe once PQ); replace pairing-based credentials (BBS+).

subsections.one_liner: Authorizing protocol and treasury decisions.
subsections.what_it_is: How protocol and treasury decisions get authorized: on-chain votes and delegations, plus off-chain signed ballots (e.g. Snapshot).
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: Voting authorization uses PQ account signatures.

cores.name: Apps & Inputs
cores.label: The programs, and the outside data they trust
cores.notes: Oracle and randomness keys are shared/threshold, so one break is systemic across all consumers. Solana PoH is a hash chain (Grover-only), not a Shor-exposed VDF.

subsections.one_liner: Contracts that check signatures.
subsections.what_it_is: A smart contract that checks a signature before acting — token approvals and gasless permits (ecrecover, EIP-712) and N-of-M multisig wallets like Safe.
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: PQ-signature precompiles/verifiers, and contracts that don't hardcode ECDSA.

subsections.one_liner: Contracts that verify ZK proofs or pairings.
subsections.what_it_is: On-chain code that verifies a cryptographic proof: the precompiles and verifier contracts that validate ZK-SNARKs (Groth16, PLONK-KZG) using elliptic-curve pairings.
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: Verify hash-based proofs (STARK/FRI) or lattice proofs instead of pairing SNARKs.

subsections.one_liner: Signed off-chain data brought on-chain.
subsections.what_it_is: Off-chain data such as asset prices brought on-chain, vouched for by operators' signatures (e.g. Chainlink OCR).
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: Reports signed/aggregated with PQ schemes. Shared keys make a break systemic.

subsections.one_liner: On-chain randomness with a proof.
subsections.what_it_is: On-chain randomness that ships with a proof it wasn't tampered with — verifiable random functions (VRFs) and threshold randomness beacons like drand.
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: PQ VRFs are research-stage; the robust option today is hash commit-reveal (RANDAO-style).

subsections.one_liner: A forced delay for fair or unbiased ordering.
subsections.what_it_is: A function that provably takes a fixed amount of sequential time to compute (a verifiable delay function), used to force fair ordering or unbiased randomness.
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: Only hash-based/sequential VDFs are a PQ candidate (weaker verification); RSA-group and class-group VDFs are breakable.

cores.name: Block Production & Proving
cores.label: How blocks get ordered, proven, and published
cores.notes: Architecture-specific: monolithic L1s (Solana, Bitcoin, Aptos/Sui) mark proof system, trusted setup, and DA commitment as N/A. KZG and pairings are the recurring vulnerability; STARK provers are PQ-safe.

subsections.one_liner: Who orders transactions into blocks.
subsections.what_it_is: Whoever decides the order of transactions in a block — an L2 sequencer, or the elected leader/proposer on an L1 — and signs off on the resulting batch.
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: The batch/block signing key is PQ. (Monolithic L1 = the leader; a separate sequencer = N/A.)

subsections.one_liner: The proof that execution was done correctly.
subsections.what_it_is: The cryptographic proof that a batch of transactions was executed correctly: a validity proof (ZK) or a fraud proof (optimistic).
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: Use a hash-based system (STARK/FRI = safe); pairing SNARKs (Groth16, PLONK-KZG) are broken; lattice commitments emerging.

subsections.one_liner: A one-time ceremony some proof systems need.
subsections.what_it_is: A one-time ceremony some proof systems need to generate public parameters, where a secret value must be destroyed afterward (powers-of-tau, the KZG reference string).
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: Use a transparent setup (STARKs need none). KZG secret is recoverable by Shor from the public parameters regardless of honest deletion.

subsections.one_liner: Proof that block data was actually published.
subsections.what_it_is: A short commitment proving a block's full data was actually published, so anyone can download it and rebuild the chain's state.
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: Hash-based commitment (Merkle/NMT = safe, e.g. Celestia); KZG-based DA (Ethereum blobs, EigenDA, Avail) is broken.

subsections.one_liner: The accumulator committing to all chain state.
subsections.what_it_is: The single cryptographic fingerprint — the root of the state tree — that commits to every balance and contract on the chain.
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: Hash-based tree (Merkle-Patricia, binary Merkle = safe); EC vector commitments (Verkle/IPA) are broken.

subsections.one_liner: Running the transactions themselves.
subsections.what_it_is: The virtual machine that actually runs transactions and smart-contract logic (e.g. the EVM).
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: Nothing of its own; signature/pairing checks belong to Apps & Inputs.

cores.name: Consensus, Finality & Cross-chain
cores.label: How the network agrees, and how chains talk
cores.notes: Shared trust pattern: verify a set of signatures from a validator set or committee, then accept state. BLS aggregation has no clean PQ analog (biggest lift). On a monolithic L1, settlement = consensus (N/A).

subsections.one_liner: Signatures that agree on and finalize blocks.
subsections.what_it_is: The signed votes validators cast to agree on blocks and mark them final — block proposals plus finality-gadget votes (Ethereum FFG = BLS, Polkadot GRANDPA = Ed25519, BEEFY = ECDSA).
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: PQ validator signatures plus a PQ way to aggregate them (no BLS drop-in; hash-based sigs + SNARK aggregation, prototype).

subsections.one_liner: Cheap verification of another chain's headers.
subsections.what_it_is: A small, rotating set of validators whose signatures let lightweight clients trust block headers without replaying the whole chain (e.g. Ethereum's sync committee).
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: PQ header signatures, or hash-based/STARK consensus proofs.

subsections.one_liner: An L2 posting its proof/state to a base chain.
subsections.what_it_is: An L2 posting its proofs and state to its base layer — the canonical bridge, made of an L1 verifier contract plus the operators authorized to update it.
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: PQ verifier + operator keys; inherits the base chain's crypto. (Monolithic L1 = N/A.)

subsections.one_liner: Moving assets and messages across chains.
subsections.what_it_is: Systems that move assets and messages between chains, secured either by an external signing committee / MPC (Wormhole, LayerZero) or by on-chain light clients (IBC).
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: PQ committee signatures or PQ light-client verification. Crypto's most-hacked surface.

subsections.one_liner: Borrowed security from a restaked operator set.
subsections.what_it_is: Operators who re-pledge their existing stake to secure extra services, signing attestations that back them (EigenLayer, Symbiotic).
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: PQ operator signatures.

subsections.one_liner: Off-chain transfers and cross-chain swaps.
subsections.what_it_is: Off-chain or conditional transfers settled by cryptographic conditions — payment channels (Lightning / HTLCs), adaptor signatures, and cross-chain atomic swaps.
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: PQ signatures, and the base chain must support them; can't migrate without the L1's signature upgrade.

subsections.one_liner: The block-building market's signatures.
subsections.what_it_is: The market that builds blocks on a proposer's behalf — relays and builders whose signed commitments are trusted to deliver and pay for a block (MEV-Boost, PBS).
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: PQ relay/builder/proposer signatures.

cores.name: Networking & Confidentiality
cores.label: The wires between the computers
cores.notes: A public ledger's data isn't secret, so harvest-now-decrypt-later mainly threatens private data, transport, node impersonation, and retroactive de-anonymization. Migrates with the broader PQ-TLS rollout.

subsections.one_liner: Encrypted links between nodes.
subsections.what_it_is: The encrypted links nodes use to gossip blocks and transactions to each other (TLS, QUIC, the Noise protocol).
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: PQ/hybrid key-exchange (ML-KEM, e.g. X25519MLKEM768).

subsections.one_liner: Keys that identify peers and validators.
subsections.what_it_is: The keypairs that identify each node and validator on the network, so peers know who they're talking to (e.g. libp2p peer IDs).
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: PQ node identity keys.

subsections.one_liner: Wallet/dapp to node connections.
subsections.what_it_is: The connections wallets and dapps use to reach the chain — HTTPS/TLS to RPC providers and relays like WalletConnect.
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: PQ/hybrid TLS on RPC endpoints and relays.

subsections.one_liner: Shared secrets derived on-chain.
subsections.what_it_is: Shared secrets derived between sender and recipient to power privacy features like stealth addresses (ERC-5564), built on Diffie-Hellman key agreement.
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: PQ key-encapsulation (ML-KEM); HNDL here retroactively de-anonymizes past activity.

subsections.one_liner: Encrypted data, memos, and private-tx systems.
subsections.what_it_is: Encryption above the transport layer: encrypted mempools (e.g. Shutter), privacy-focused chains, and off-chain encrypted data stores.
subsections.quantum_proof_requirement: PQ encryption/threshold schemes; FHE (lattice) is already PQ, but pairing-SNARK wrappers may not be.
